  6. @ExposingUFOs: Karma is just Reddit’s social credit system. There’s upvotes and downvotes. 
People upvote what they like/agree with and downvote what they dislike/disagree with.

Your comment Karma is your total amount of upvotes minus your total amount of downvotes on comments.
Similarly, post Karma is upvotes minus downvotes on posts specifically.

Your Karma goes up & down accordingly. Karma is not static.

- Is OnlyFans Safe? - Trend Unwrapper: Kind: captions Language: en Is Only Fans safe? When it comes to using Only Fans, many people wonder, is it safe? This social media platform has gained popularity for allowing creators to share content directly with subscribers who pay for access. Unlike traditional social networks, Only Fans has a unique business model. Creators earn money through subscriptions and tips instead of relying on advertisements. This has made it a standout option among niche social media platforms. From a safety standpoint, Only Fans is generally considered safe. Much like other mainstream social networks, the platform operates as a legitimate business and has put measures in place to protect user privacy and data. However, it is important to know that like any online service that involves money and personal information, there are some risks for creators. There are potential scams or unauthorized sharing of content. Users may face risks such as account hacking or exposure of payment details. To help mitigate these risks, Only Fans collects personal data to verify identities and protect the community. This verification process helps reduce fraud and maintain a safer environment for both creators and subscribers. Only Fans also provides tools for users to manage their privacy settings and report inappropriate behavior. This is essential for creating a secure space for everyone involved. In the context of cultural trends, Only Fans represents a shift toward more direct creator-driven content sharing. It reflects the growing demand for platforms that allow individuals to monetize their influence or creative output without relying on traditional media. Practical safety measures for users include setting strong passwords and enabling two factor authentication. It is also wise to be cautious about sharing personal information. For creators, watermarking content and being selective about what is shared publicly can help protect their work. Both users and creators should regularly review their privacy settings and stay informed about platform updates. While Only Fans is mostly safe, it is not entirely risk-f free. Being aware of potential threats and taking proactive safety measures can help users and creators enjoy the platform while minimizing exposure to harm. The rise of Only Fans highlights changing attitudes toward digital content creation and consumption. More people are looking for ways to support creators directly and access content that is not available on mainstream platforms.

Now the company wants to build a different kind of social media platform, one that's not reliant on advertising and personalized algorithms. Key Blair, CEO of Only Fans, sat down with the Wall Street Journal to discuss her plan to build the world's safest digital platform and why she thinks some banks still use an outdated risk model. What did you do for work before you joined Only Fans and how did you land here? My only fans origin story is quite different from that of most UK um and US tech CEOs. So before I joined Only Fans, I was a partner at a law firm and I led their cyber security, online safety and privacy practice. So not the typical background for a tech company CEO. Search function is a great example of one of those things. So we don't have highly personalized content algorithms on on only fans. You're not going to get a bunch of suggested creators that is specially curated for you to try and encourage you to spend more time on the platform because we believe that users as well as creators should also have control. So users should be able to choose who they subscribe to. You shouldn't be bombarded with content constantly trying to keep you online more. Same with no advertising. Same with no data monetization. In order to search for a creator on Only Fans, you need to know their exact username to be able to find them. You can't search by genre. You can't search by like first name or last name. The only thing you can search for is their username. And the reason for that is actually because we found historically across the internet that when broad search terms are applied, people can seek to abuse those to promote content that is illegal or harmful or things along those lines. - What Is OnlyFans? Here’s What Every Parent Needs to Know: Kind: captions Language: en (calm music) - Have you heard of OnlyFans? It's been in the news recently, but many folks may not know exactly what it is or how dangerous it can be for children. Hi, I'm Caroline, a Senior Growth Marketing Manager at Bark, a parental controls company. Today, I'm going to explain how OnlyFans works, why it's so popular, and why it's definitely not for kids. OnlyFans is a website where people can post exclusive content that others can subscribe to. It has more than 130 million users and over a million content creators. Top earners on the platform can make hundreds of thousands of dollars each month. In terms of what the site looks like, think of it kind of like Facebook, but in order for someone to see your messages, photos, or videos, they'd have to pay you $5 a month or some other monthly price. Creators can upload videos they've filmed or they can broadcast live to their fans. They can also choose to provide their content in the subscription or pay-per-view model. But you're probably wondering, why would someone want to pay money for content when other sites like Facebook, YouTube, Instagram, and Spotify offer it for free? The answer is - it's complicated. There are plenty of content creators on OnlyFans that don't have anything to do with sex - like celebrities, musicians, and fitness instructors - but a lot of what draws subscribers to the site is adult content. In 2021, the site announced that it was going to ban explicit content, and quickly backtracked that decision, primarily because sexual content is what keeps the site afloat. Officially, you have to be 18 or older to sign up for an OnlyFans account because of all the sexual content. To subscribe to pages as a fan, even free accounts, you're asked to add a credit card. This is meant to help weed out kids, but it doesn't always work. Personal credit cards, prepaid debit cards, or even an unsuspecting parent's cards are often used to get past the restriction. To become a creator on OnlyFans, the verification process is more intense. This makes sense because creators will be making money from their content, which could include sexual activity. To share content, you're required to connect a bank account to receive payment. Then, to confirm your age, a third-party company takes a picture of your face. This will have to match to a form of ID you upload like a passport or driver's license. As you may have guessed though, this doesn't mean that it's impossible for minors to create an account. Or handed down, and kids can obtain bank accounts. If a child somehow manages to bypass all the restrictions and become an OnlyFans creator, they may face many dangers. They'll be able to post photos or videos that could be copied, downloaded, and shared in different places, all over the world, potentially forever. On the platform itself, kids could be contacted by other OnlyFans users and their subscribers, which could lead to predation, sextortion, and more, including aggressive and threatening messages from so-called fans. While OnlyFans consists primarily of amateur creators, the site is buzzworthy enough to have attracted the attention of quite a few celebrities. Cardi B has an account, as do pop culture figures like Aaron Carter, Amber Rose, DJ Khalid, and more. Some share adult content, but not all. Part of the appeal of celebrities having OnlyFans accounts is that fans get exclusive access to their lives that casual followers, say on Twitter or Instagram, don't get. Recently, the rapper Bhad Bhabie, you may remember from the "catch me outside" meme a few years back, made over $1 million in just six hours. We hope this video has helped you better understand what OnlyFans is as well as all of the dangers it presents to kids. If you're worried about your child stumbling across this website, Bark can help. That's&nbsp;&nbsp; big finance cutting off the business entirely,&nbsp; forcing it to change. It's also worth considering&nbsp;&nbsp; why adult platforms get treated as high risk in&nbsp; the first place. Sure, the bank gets some of the&nbsp;&nbsp; platform's upside, but it also gets a lot of nasty&nbsp; downside risk, too. That means elevated fraud and&nbsp;&nbsp; refund disputes, higher chargeback exposure,&nbsp; and above all else, reputational and regulatory&nbsp;&nbsp; blowback if illegal exploitation slips through.&nbsp; So, this isn't just some straightforward PR stuff.&nbsp;&nbsp; Mastercard's published network rules clearly&nbsp; outline strict compliance expectations, including&nbsp;&nbsp; anti-moneylaundering and sanctions requirements,&nbsp; plus how non-compliance gets assessed. Visa is&nbsp;&nbsp; much the same way, and there's also a lot of&nbsp; pressure on banks as a result. In this dynamic,&nbsp;&nbsp; the bank is responsible for keeping merchants&nbsp; inside the lines. Now, if you think this is&nbsp;&nbsp; standard business compliance stuff, well, just&nbsp; take a look back to 2021. In August that year,&nbsp;&nbsp; Only Fans announced that it would ban content&nbsp; showing sexually explicit conduct. And it clearly&nbsp;&nbsp; said the change was to satisfy banking partners&nbsp; and payment providers. That set off immediate&nbsp;&nbsp; creator backlash because, of course, explicit&nbsp; content was the core business. And not even a&nbsp;&nbsp; week later, Only Fans reverse course. The platform&nbsp; said it had secured the needed assurances to keep&nbsp;&nbsp; supporting adult content. So, all's well that ends&nbsp; well, right? Well, fast forward to just last year,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the scrutiny hasn't gone away. In 2025,&nbsp; a whistleblower complaint to Fininsen alleged&nbsp;&nbsp; that Mastercard and Visa failed to stop their&nbsp; networks from being used to process payments&nbsp;&nbsp; linked to child sex abuse and sex trafficking&nbsp; on Only Fans. Mastercard and Visa disputed the&nbsp;&nbsp; allegations and Only Fans denied tolerating&nbsp; illegal content. But the insight here is that&nbsp;&nbsp; this pressure hits the payment infrastructure&nbsp; first and then ricochets into platform policy.&nbsp;&nbsp; Only Fans relies very heavily on card payments to&nbsp; fuel its billion-dollar business. Any issue there&nbsp;&nbsp; and it's over. As we've seen, major decisions&nbsp; impacting this multi-billion dollar business&nbsp;&nbsp; can be made overnight. But keeping up with&nbsp; all of these market girrations can be tough&nbsp;&nbsp; unless you've signed up for the free Coinbureau&nbsp; Finance newsletter. That's where we break down&nbsp;&nbsp; the key moves across businesses, markets,&nbsp; politics, and more, so you stay informed&nbsp;&nbsp; on what matters. We also have exclusive deals&nbsp; for Coin Bureau Finance viewers. Discounts,&nbsp;&nbsp; bonuses, and promotions put together exclusively&nbsp; for viewers of this channel. All you have to do is&nbsp;&nbsp; click the link in the description or scan this QR&nbsp; code. And now back to the video. Okay, let's pivot&nbsp;&nbsp; for a moment from the platform side of things&nbsp; to the creators that actually make all of the&nbsp;&nbsp; site's content. The scale on the creator side is&nbsp; frankly insane. In the latest published numbers,&nbsp;&nbsp; that's for 2024, creator accounts rose 13% to&nbsp; about 4.6 million. Fan accounts climbed to roughly&nbsp;&nbsp; $377 million and subscriber spending hit $7.2&nbsp; billion for the year. That resulted in about $5.8&nbsp;&nbsp; billion paid out to creators under the platform's&nbsp; 80/20 split. Now, you may have noticed something&nbsp;&nbsp; a bit odd there. So, let's dig into those numbers&nbsp; a bit. If you just divide that $5.8 billion by 4.6&nbsp;&nbsp; million creators, you get roughly $1,260 per&nbsp; creator per year. That number isn't a precise&nbsp;&nbsp; average paycheck. Of course, no, it's a flashing&nbsp; warning signal that this is a winner takemost&nbsp;&nbsp; market where a tiny cohort of creators harvests a&nbsp; huge share of the money and a very large crowd is&nbsp;&nbsp; fighting for leftovers. Now, of course, that kind&nbsp; of concentration isn't unique to Only Fans. It's a&nbsp;&nbsp; defining feature of the broader creator economy.&nbsp; Last year, one report from Creator IQ found the&nbsp;&nbsp; top creators are capturing an increasing share of&nbsp; payouts, while median outcomes lagged hard. Now,&nbsp;&nbsp; Only Fans doesn't publish a percentile split of&nbsp; its creators, but independent analysis from Only&nbsp;&nbsp; Gaider estimates the top 0.1% of creators capture&nbsp; about 76% of revenue share. Sounds lucrative for&nbsp;&nbsp; the top earners. So, what is the actual job&nbsp; description for an Only Fans creator? And no,&nbsp;&nbsp; keep your mind out of the gutter. Well, the idea&nbsp; is to build and run a subscription business where&nbsp;&nbsp; your product is access. That means constant&nbsp; traffic generation offplatform while focusing&nbsp;&nbsp; on increasing retention on platform and a sales&nbsp; system that resembles customer support with some&nbsp;&nbsp; flirting attached. There's no algorithm that&nbsp; reliably hands you customers. In academic work&nbsp;&nbsp; analyzing the platform, yes, it does exist.&nbsp; Researchers show how creators end up leaning&nbsp;&nbsp; heavily on third-party social networks to promote&nbsp; themselves and interact with followers. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; that sounds like a lot of work for one person to&nbsp; handle. And well, that's why we have the rise of&nbsp;&nbsp; only fans agencies. If accounts manage to scale,&nbsp; then creators hit a hard ceiling. They can't film,&nbsp;&nbsp; edit, post, market, upsell, and message hundreds&nbsp; of people all day without either burning out or&nbsp;&nbsp; building a team. Agencies, however, can run the&nbsp; whole operation as they can manage the account,&nbsp;&nbsp; promote it on social media, organize custom&nbsp; content sales, and even hire so-called chatters&nbsp;&nbsp; who handle paid for private messages while posing&nbsp; as the creator. Some creators have told reporters&nbsp;&nbsp; that they barely log in at all. They send content&nbsp; to the agency and let the machine do its thing.&nbsp;&nbsp; Crazy. And that whole agency layer turns Only&nbsp; Fans into something bigger than just a platform.&nbsp;&nbsp; It becomes an entire labor market ecosystem.&nbsp; Those networks of outsourced chat workers are a&nbsp;&nbsp; particularly bizarre feature. Typically outsourced&nbsp; to emerging market countries like the Philippines.&nbsp;&nbsp; These chatters even have sales quotas except the&nbsp; job is to sell intimacy. But even if a creator&nbsp;&nbsp; never hires an agency, the market itself can&nbsp; still get tough and many creators argue that&nbsp;&nbsp; it is. Some have talked to reporters about&nbsp; market saturation, relentless content demands,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the mental strain of having your income tied&nbsp; to staying on for subscribers every single day.&nbsp;&nbsp; The average annual pay of a little over&nbsp; $1,000 is proof enough that it's not as&nbsp;&nbsp; easy as some may think. Only Fans sells creators&nbsp; the idea of independence. The reality, however,&nbsp;&nbsp; is learning the same lesson that every small&nbsp; business learns. It's very difficult to succeed&nbsp;&nbsp; and it's very time consuming too. Okay, now&nbsp; that's the supply side so to speak, but what&nbsp;&nbsp; about the demand side? As we briefly mentioned&nbsp; earlier, Only Fans consumers are paying for more&nbsp;&nbsp; than just content. They're paying for access and&nbsp; attention on demand. They're paying for something&nbsp;&nbsp; that feels more direct and private instead of&nbsp; something mass-produced and tossed into a feed&nbsp;&nbsp; with some ads sprinkled on top. So, the standard&nbsp; subscription model is only half the story. Over&nbsp;&nbsp; recent years, Only Fan CEO Key Blair has said&nbsp; that payments on the platform have been shifting&nbsp;&nbsp; from a traditional subscription first model and&nbsp; towards microtransactions. These are the one-off&nbsp;&nbsp; purchases that happen inside the relationship once&nbsp; someone is already through the door. Economically,&nbsp;&nbsp; that shift is a big deal because these&nbsp; microtransactions essentially act as a form of&nbsp;&nbsp; price discrimination hardwired into the product.&nbsp; A subscription gets you into the room. After that,&nbsp;&nbsp; the platform lets creators charge different&nbsp; customers different amounts for different&nbsp;&nbsp; levels of access without needing complicated tiers&nbsp; or bundling. You can be a customer that spends a&nbsp;&nbsp; fixed $10 per month, or you can be a $10 a month&nbsp; plus another $200 this weekend type of fella. So,&nbsp;&nbsp; different customers represent completely different&nbsp; revenue profiles for creators. And don't let the&nbsp;&nbsp; micro in microtransactions fool you. These can&nbsp; still get expensive for customers. Whether each&nbsp;&nbsp; transaction carries a considerable price or the&nbsp; small charges quickly add up. Creators can payw&nbsp;&nbsp; wall their entire profile with monthly prices&nbsp; as is familiar in the modern-day subscription&nbsp;&nbsp; economy. Then they can layer on paid posts,&nbsp; messages, and other one-off unlocks that can&nbsp;&nbsp; run up to triple digits depending on settings and&nbsp; limits. And for many creators, this appears to be&nbsp;&nbsp; the major monetization avenue. In October 2025,&nbsp; Blair said Only Fans had paid out $25 billion to&nbsp;&nbsp; creators since 2016. That kind of cumulative&nbsp; payout is only possible if a massive base of&nbsp;&nbsp; customers keeps choosing to pay for something&nbsp; they could technically get elsewhere for free.&nbsp;&nbsp; So, this all begs the question, where are all&nbsp; these customers coming from? Now, we don't&nbsp;&nbsp; get a neat public breakdown of fan spending by&nbsp; country, but web traffic gives a decent proxy&nbsp;&nbsp; for where attention is concentrated. Seamrush's&nbsp; January 2026 snapshot put onlyfans.com at around&nbsp;&nbsp; 400 million visits for the month with the United&nbsp; States at around 40% of visits followed by the UK,&nbsp;&nbsp; Mexico, Germany, and Brazil. Notably, more than&nbsp; half the traffic was shown to be direct visits,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is consistent with what a uh habit looks&nbsp; like on the internet. So, that's the demand base.&nbsp;&nbsp; You're dealing with a platform that's not a random&nbsp; website. people stumble across but a destination&nbsp;&nbsp; people go back to. Now this is in part due to&nbsp; some constraints that only fans faces. They can't&nbsp;&nbsp; launch mobile apps on leading app stores due to&nbsp; the nature of most of the content for instance.&nbsp;&nbsp; But the huge dollar numbers and loyal customer&nbsp; base only fans sees are where the platform starts&nbsp;&nbsp; to become useful as an economic indicator. Of&nbsp; course, we're not talking about a precise macro&nbsp;&nbsp; gauge. The best internal metrics are private and&nbsp; public proxies like web traffic don't tell you&nbsp;&nbsp; whether people are spending more or just browsing.&nbsp; But only fans does sit right at the intersection&nbsp;&nbsp; of two things that move with the economy.&nbsp; Discretionary spending and personal financial&nbsp;&nbsp; pressure. If consumers start tightening up,&nbsp; platforms living on recurring payments and add-on&nbsp;&nbsp; purchases should feel it. One 2025 survey from&nbsp; Deote found people aren't increasing what they&nbsp;&nbsp; spend on subscriptions and many report fatigue&nbsp; managing multiple subscriptions alongside rising&nbsp;&nbsp; prices. Another survey from Boston Consulting&nbsp; Group found that consumers expect to spend&nbsp;&nbsp; more from inflation, not desire. So many people&nbsp; are planning cutbacks on all manner of consumer&nbsp;&nbsp; goods and services. According to that survey,&nbsp; confidence has turned significantly negative in&nbsp;&nbsp; developed markets since June 2025. So while Only&nbsp; Fans figures have been on the rise, at least until&nbsp;&nbsp; the last reported numbers for 2024, if we begin&nbsp; to see revenue dropping, it's a signal that the&nbsp;&nbsp; average Joe is struggling. In the K-shaped economy&nbsp; we're now faced with, higher inome households can&nbsp;&nbsp; keep spending while everyone else has been&nbsp; getting squeezed by essentials like rent and&nbsp;&nbsp; food. And many would argue that squeeze is visible&nbsp; in the explosive rise of women using Only Fans as&nbsp;&nbsp; a side hustle. This even includes students who've&nbsp; explicitly said they're using it to cover tuition&nbsp;&nbsp; and basic living costs. At the risk of sounding&nbsp; judgmental, that's not exactly a pretty picture of&nbsp;&nbsp; the economy. But while people use Only Fan stats&nbsp; as a proxy measure of the economy's poor health,&nbsp;&nbsp; the Only Fans economic model itself faces some&nbsp; potential issues. What's the biggest threat? Well,&nbsp;&nbsp; everyone's favorite thing right now. That's&nbsp; right, AI. AI is the first technology that&nbsp;&nbsp; can mass-produce the Only Fans type creator and&nbsp; that can disrupt the Only Fans economy. And that&nbsp;&nbsp; story begins with theft. If someone can scrape a&nbsp; creator's photos, swap a face, clone their voice,&nbsp;&nbsp; and generate new clips through AI tech, you get&nbsp; counterfeit supply that competes with the real&nbsp;&nbsp; account. And because deep fakes spread fastest&nbsp; offplatform, the damage can happen before a&nbsp;&nbsp; creator even notices. That's creepy enough as it&nbsp; is, and it's a serious violation of the creator's&nbsp;&nbsp; personal autonomy. But the structural threat from&nbsp; AI isn't just impersonations or ripoffs. It's&nbsp;&nbsp; original content. Over the last year, AI generated&nbsp; influencers have become conversion tools. Yes,&nbsp;&nbsp; synthetic models running Instagram accounts&nbsp; that pull people into paid adult subscription&nbsp;&nbsp; sites. The so-called AI pimping scene describes&nbsp; an ecosystem of accounts mixing fully AI images&nbsp;&nbsp; with deep faked videos, then monetizing through&nbsp; subscription links. And once the paying customer&nbsp;&nbsp; lands on the subscription page, they're often&nbsp; buying more AI content, not a human creator at&nbsp;&nbsp; all. And that's where things get really weird.&nbsp; A synthetic only fans type model doesn't burn&nbsp;&nbsp; out and can be scaled like software. Fan view, an&nbsp; adultonly subscription platform that leans into&nbsp;&nbsp; AI, has stated that AI creators have accounted&nbsp; for up to 15% of its revenues during certain&nbsp;&nbsp; months. They also highlighted an AI persona that&nbsp; generated about $23,000 in a single month after&nbsp;&nbsp; a rapid ramp. And if the creator is synthetic, the&nbsp; operator can be anyone, including some random dude&nbsp;&nbsp; running prompts and DMs. The product becomes a&nbsp; character and the work becomes conversion and&nbsp;&nbsp; retention that is entirely detached from any real&nbsp; person whatsoever. And AI isn't only an outside&nbsp;&nbsp; threat. It's already being used inside the creator&nbsp; economy to scale the private communication part of&nbsp;&nbsp; the job that we mentioned earlier. Some models&nbsp; and agencies are using AI to keep up with DMs,&nbsp;&nbsp; replacing or supplementing the humans who&nbsp; used to do that work. For human creators,&nbsp;&nbsp; all of this results in a new kind of competition&nbsp; accounts that can flood the market with infinite&nbsp;&nbsp; custom content priced aggressively and constantly&nbsp; optimized. Of course, one obvious response is to&nbsp;&nbsp; sell proof of reality, whether that's something&nbsp; like a live interaction or verified identity. But&nbsp;&nbsp; that increases workload and raises the bar&nbsp; for everyone. Only fans as a platform can't&nbsp;&nbsp; ignore this either, and not just because they may&nbsp; lose revenue to other platforms that embrace and&nbsp;&nbsp; leverage unscrupulous AI content creators. This&nbsp; all comes with increased legal and operational&nbsp;&nbsp; cost. The US Take It Down Act pushes hard for fast&nbsp; removal expectations for non-consensual intimate&nbsp;&nbsp; imagery, including AI fakes. This may also explain&nbsp; why Only Fans has been trying to adjust its image,&nbsp;&nbsp; even if just slightly. If you listen to their&nbsp; executives, Only Fans wishes to be seen as a&nbsp;&nbsp; creator platform, not just an adult content site.&nbsp; CEO Key Blair has argued it can be a major player&nbsp;&nbsp; in the broader creator economy, pitching safety&nbsp; and monetization across categories, maybe, but&nbsp;&nbsp; the brand association is sticky to say the least.&nbsp; And the same bank and card network constraints&nbsp;&nbsp; apply. Even if Only Fans wants to onboard a more&nbsp; diverse range of creators, a lot of creators with&nbsp;&nbsp; mainstream sponsors may decide the reputational&nbsp; risk just isn't worth it. So although Only Fans&nbsp;&nbsp; owners might be ready to sell, negotiations around&nbsp; valuation may prove to be tough. All right, folks.&nbsp;&nbsp; And that's about all we have time for today. But&nbsp; what do you think? Will Only Fans keep putting up&nbsp;&nbsp; huge numbers? Does AI actually pose a threat,&nbsp; or is that just speculation going wild? - The Disturbing Business of OnlyFans Explained in 11 Minutes: Kind: captions Language: en Quiet Launch Only Fans launches in London in 2016. Created by Tim Stokeley, a serial entrepreneur focused on niche digital platforms. His idea is simple but ambitious, a direct subscription service where creators can charge fans for access to exclusive posts, messages, or videos. The parent company, Phoenix International, backs the project, aiming to capture revenue from a growing creator economy still dominated by ad-driven models like YouTube and Instagram. Unlike free platforms, Only Fans promotes itself as a paid alternative that allows fitness trainers, lifestyle coaches, and even amateur chefs to monetize their expertise. In the first two years, growth is limited as the platform attracts mainly small communities searching for closer fan engagement rather than mass audiences. Creators experiment with cooking tutorials, personalized workout plans, and behind-the-scenes lifestyle updates, but the model struggles to break beyond niche markets. Stokeley brands the site as a hybrid of Patreon and social media, promising patronage meets social media in his own words, but without a breakthrough use case. Adoption remains slow. Pandemic boom. In early 2020, global lockdowns trigger one of the fastest growth surges in Only Fans history. Traffic across the platform spikes 75% between March and April. Driven by people stuck at home searching for both entertainment and income. Creator numbers expand at unprecedented speed, growing from several hundred thousand in 2019 to over 1 million by the end of 2020. Meanwhile, users climb even faster with total registered accounts swelling to roughly 85 million before 2020 closes. This wave of adoption generates massive cash flows almost overnight. By late 2020, monthly payouts to creators exceed $200 million compared to relatively modest sums just a year earlier. The rising unemployment normalizes the platform as a legitimate financial lifeline. Media outlets start labeling Only Fans the pandemic success story, which helps fuel further signups, but also hardens its image as a company reliant on extraordinary circumstances. With explosive numbers now locked in, the question shifts to what kind of content is driving most of this activity shift. By 2019, the direction of Only Fans is no longer ambiguous. Adult content begins to dominate subscriptions with the majority of growth tied directly to explicit material. Unlike its early experiments with fitness and cooking tutorials, the platform now becomes the preferred marketplace for creators openly selling sexual content to paying fans. The subscription model proves especially lucrative for this category because it offers creators a reliable monthly income instead of unpredictable ad revenue. This shift fundamentally reshapes the brand's reputation in the market, making it synonymous with pornography rather than the broader creator economy. The turning point comes in 2020 when actress Bella Thorne joins the platform, drawing mass media coverage. Within 24 hours, she earns $1 million from subscriptions, an event that demonstrates the platform's earning potential, but also exposes its vulnerabilities. Fans soon discover that the highly priced pay-per-view posts advertised as nude are in fact non-explicit photos. Feeling misled, thousands request refunds, triggering mass chargebacks that disrupt payment processors and prompt only fans to impose new limits on pricing, tipping, and payouts. Independent creators, who now face delayed payments because of stricter rules, accuse Thorne of damaging the trust and economic stability of the community that built the platform. As frustration spreads, mainstream backlash accelerates as journalists label only fans a portite in disguise and the sudden influx of celebrity accounts heightens the tension. Non- adult creators feel squeezed out, unable to compete with the traffic and spending directed toward explicit content. This dynamic cementss Only Fans reliance on adult material, which becomes both its primary driver of revenue and its greatest regulatory liability. Issues that come to the surface once cash surges into the business. billion flood baby. Only Fans records extraordinary financial growth. By 2021, total transactions processed through the platform reached nearly 5 billion, placing Only Fans firmly among the most profitable subscription services on the internet. Because the company takes a 20% cut of every payment. This translated into close to $1 billion in annual revenue flowing directly to its parent company, Phoenix International. The financial upside also extends to top creators with a small elite earning over $10 million annually, while broader payouts surpassed $5 billion cumulatively by 2023. Banking crackdown. In 2021, Only Fans confronts its most serious business challenge when banks and payment processors like BNY Melon, JP Morgan Chase, and Metro Bank tighten compliance rules for adult content transactions. These new standards force platforms hosting explicit material to demonstrate tougher monitoring and verification systems before payments can be processed. Because Only Fans depends entirely on these financial networks, the management overreacted by announcing a ban on sexually explicit content in August 2021. The decision immediately jeopardizes the company's primary revenue stream since adult material accounts for the overwhelming majority of subscriptions. The response from creators is swift. Outrage spreads across social media as creators denounce the ban and warn they will migrate to competing platforms if the policy remains in place. Within a week, the company reverses its decision, stating that it has secured assurances from banking partners, but the damage is clear. The episode exposes how fragile the business is, especially when payment processors or financial institutions reassess risk. Investors take note with many describing the company's dependency on adult content as a poor risk too unstable for long-term growth. That perception of instability fuels further leadership changes at the top. 120 million goodbye. In December 2021, Tim Stokeley, the founder and chief executive of Only Fans, steps down from his role after 5 years of leading the platform's explosive growth. His resignation comes just months after the controversy over the attempted explicit content ban, which raised questions about the company's long-term stability. Stokeley frames the decision as making way for younger leadership, though he departs with an estimated personal fortune of $120 million from his stake in the company. Ammy Gan, the company's former chief marketing and communications officer, replaces Stokeley as chief executive, becoming the public face of the platform at a critical juncture. Supporters present the leadership transition as a modernization step, but critics interpret it as an effort to shield the company from further scrutiny after a tumultuous year. GAN inherits the challenge of preserving billions in creator payouts while addressing ongoing regulatory and reputational risks. With strategy unclear and leadership untested, the shift raises doubts about Only Fans ability to maintain momentum. That uncertainty only sharpens when closer attention is paid to the conditions facing the majority of its creators. Where income distribution and workload reveal a very different reality, creator grind. Behind the billiondoll payouts and celebrity success stories, most creators on Only Fans earn far less than headlines suggest. Reports estimate that the average monthly income per creator is roughly $150 to $180, a fraction of what top performers make. In fact, the top 1% of accounts capture roughly onethird of all earnings on the platform, leaving the vast majority with far smaller shares. This extreme inequality forces many small creators to churn out constant content, respond to private messages, and maintain a so-called girlfriend experience simply to retain subscribers. The workload often extends far beyond what is visible to paying fans. Creators describe managing unpaid tasks like moderating their own content, handling disputes, and dealing with the risk of chargebacks. Many quit within 6 months after realizing the time demands outweigh the modest financial return, while others face burnout from the pressure of being constantly available. Mental health strain is rarely acknowledged by the company, even as it markets itself as an engine of financial empowerment. This growing disconnect between Only Fans profitability and the reality for most of its creators complicates the platform's public image, an image the company works hard to reframe through strategic partnerships and celebrity endorsements. Cleanup game Only Fans invests heavily in rebranding itself as a mainstream platform, even as the bulk of its revenue continues to come from explicit content. Corporate Communications highlight partnerships with music artists and fashion brands, positioning the site as a leader in the broader creator economy. High-profile figures such as Cardi B use the platform to promote behind-the-scenes content, carefully avoiding nudity to emphasize that it is not exclusively an adult site, aligning it more with Patreon than Despite these efforts, media coverage consistently frames Only Fans as a pornography-driven business. And on social media, Only Fans also suffers from a poor reputation due to the almost aggressive way many creators try to attract new subscribers for explicit content. To amplify visibility, many creators partner with marketing agencies that operate multiple accounts across Instagram, X, and Tik Tok. These firms often automate follower growth and direct message outreach, generating networks of near identical profiles that funnel users towards subscription links. But those same agencies don't just grow the audience. They often become the creator, taking over private messages, flirting, and upselling content to fans who believe they're talking to the real person. As a result, social media becomes saturated with promotional posts and link in bio accounts. Some operated by real creators, others by bots, blurring the line between authentic engagement and algorithmic advertising. This erosion of trust spills over into broader debates about platform safety, where regulators amplify concerns about exploitation. Regulators and watchdog groups have raised concerns about underage users attempting to access the platform, increasing public skepticism about its safeguards. The resulting tugof-war leaves the company stuck between two identities. A tech startup claiming cultural legitimacy and an adult industry player under constant scrutiny. This unresolved tension complicates investor interest and raises questions about long-term viability. Those questions become sharper as financial disclosures show strong profitability, but also highlight the looming risks of regulation, competition, and dependence on volatile banking relationships. One ban away. By 2022, Only Fans reported strong profitability, placing it among the most lucrative subscription platforms relative to its size. Strong earnings spark recurring rumors of an eventual IPO. But investment banks hesitate to support a listing tied so closely to adult content. Financial institutions were seen as cautious, citing reputational risk, regulatory uncertainty, and the precedent set by the 2021 banking crackdown. At the same time, competition intensifies from platforms like Fansley, Patreon, and Many Vids, which attempt to lure creators with more flexible policies and fewer reputational constraints. Governments also weigh stricter age verification rules, a shift that could dramatically increase compliance costs while discouraging new users. Creators express anxiety over sudden bans or abrupt policy reversals, fearing the kind of disruption nearly triggered in 2021. That sense of instability hangs over the platform even during its peak profitability. The question remains whether only fans can evolve into a sustainable long-term business or whether its dependence on adult content leaves it perpetually one policy change away from collapse. So, what do you think about the rise and the risks of Only Fans? Do you use Only Fans yourself? If you liked this video, hit that like button and subscribe for more awesome content. See you guys.

- The Rise of OnlyFans – The Most Controversial Billion Dollar Platform .: Kind: captions Language: en Hi, I am Yamami. Welcome to my video. So, let's start. Imagine a website that started in a small home office with a tiny team. Today, it processes over $5 billion in payments every single year. It has more than 2 million creators and over 200 million users. Most people know it for its controversy, but from a financial perspective, it is one of the most efficient business models ever created. It is the story of a system that removed the middleman and changed how people trade attention for money. This is the story of Only Fans. In 2016, a British businessman named Tim Stokeley had a simple idea. At the time, social media was already giant. Platforms like Instagram and Twitter were everywhere. But there was a major problem for the people making the content. The platforms were making all the money from ads, but the creators were getting almost nothing. Tim wanted to build a payw wall for individuals. If you wanted to see someone's special photos or videos, you had to pay a monthly subscription. It was a very simple concept. Just like Phil Knight and his crazy idea for running shoes, Tim had a vision for a new kind of internet economy. He didn't have a giant office or thousands of employees at the start. He had a small loan from his father and a clear rule for his business. The creator would keep 80% of the money and the platform would keep 20%. This is what we call a commission model. It was very different from traditional media companies that might take 50 or 60% of a person's earnings. By giving more power and money to the creators, the platform began to grow incredibly fast. In the beginning, Only Fans tried to attract all kinds of creators. They looked for fitness trainers, chefs, and musicians. They wanted to be the place where any expert could charge for their knowledge. Just like a drop shipper chooses a niche to find a market, only fans was looking for its perfect audience, they found it in a way they didn't exactly plan. The adult industry saw the platform as a sanctuary. For many years, creators in that industry were treated poorly by big corporations. Only fans gave them a way to own their own business and keep most of their earnings. It gave them full control over their own production. By 2018, the platform was growing, but it needed to scale up. An entrepreneur named Leo Radvinsky bought a majority stake in the company. He understood the mindset of digital growth. He knew that in the world of software, once you have a working system, adding a million more users costs almost nothing. This is a high margin business. Nearly every extra dollar earned is pure profit because there are no physical products to ship or shoes to manufacture. Then the world changed in 2020. Just as a jogging boom once made Nike a household name, the global lockdowns made digital content the only way for people to connect. Millions of people were stuck at home. Many lost their jobs and needed a new way to earn money. Only Fans became a global phenomenon. In just one year, the amount of money flowing through the site jumped from a few hundred million to over $2 billion. It was a digital gold rush. To become truly mainstream, the platform needed big names. Just like Nike signed Michael Jordan to prove they were the best in the world, only fans began to see massive celebrities join the site. When famous actresses and musicians started making millions of dollars in a single day, the financial world stopped and took notice. It wasn't just a small website anymore. It was a multi-billion dollar empire that was disrupting the entire entertainment industry. But with massive growth comes massive trouble. In August 2021, Only Fans faced its biggest nightmare. It was a moment that almost ended the company forever. Just like Nike was targeted by the government with a $25 million tax bill, Only Fans was targeted by the global banking system. Major banks were worried about the type of content being sold on the site. They threatened to stop processing all payments. Without the ability to take credit cards, the site would die in a matter of days. The company made a shocking announcement. They said they would ban the content that made them famous to satisfy the banks. The creators felt betrayed. They felt like the company was stabbing them in the back just like a supplier might leave a partner for a better deal. But after a few days of intense pressure and a massive backlash from their users, only fans did something very bold. They stood their ground. They worked out a way to improve their safety systems and convinced the banks to stay. They reversed the ban and kept their business model alive. This legal and financial battle showed how much power the platform actually had. They had built a community that was too big for the banks to ignore. Today, the numbers are staggering. The company makes hundreds of millions of dollars in net profit every year with a relatively small team of employees. They don't have the high costs of a traditional business. They don't have factories or warehouses. They simply provide the software and the payment system. The rise of only fans teaches us a lot about modern finance and the power of the internet. It shows that the system is changing. In the past, you needed a giant company to reach a global audience. Today, you just need a phone and a platform that lets you connect with your fans. It is a bottomup strategy. By focusing on the individual creator, Only Fans built a network that is almost impossible to break. The company's success comes from a simple mindset. Reinvest in the system and keep the fees low for the users. They created a digital marketplace where people can turn their own personality into a business. Even though the platform remains very controversial, its financial success is undeniable. They have reached a level of profitability that most tech companies can only dream of. When we look back at the history of big brands, we see that they all had to face crisis and controversy. Nike had to deal with labor scandals and legal fights. Only fans has to deal with reputation issues and banking pressure. But in both cases, the companies that survive are the ones that focus on their core mission. For only fans, that mission is allowing creators to get paid directly by their fans. So, what is the lesson here? It is that the timing is never perfect, and the path to success is rarely a straight line. Tim Stokeley took a simple idea and a small loan and turned it into a billiondoll reality. He found a gap in the market and used technology to fill it. He proved that even a controversial idea can change the world. if it offers a better way for people to make a living. The creator economy is still in its early stages. Every day, more people are leaving traditional jobs to start their own digital businesses. Only Fans was one of the first platforms to show how much money is really out there for people who can build a loyal following. Whether you agree with the content or not, the financial system they built is a masterpiece of efficiency. In the end, Only Fans is a story about the power of the individual. It is about a system that rewards the person making the content instead of the giant corporation hosting it. It is a reminder that in the modern world, the most valuable thing you can own is your own audience. Just like the founders of Nike, the people behind Only Fans took a leap of faith. They didn't wait for permission. They just did it. and by doing so they built one of the most successful and talked about companies of the 21st century. But let's talk about the first most simple option, that is iMovie. If you have a Mac, it's already on your computer. You can literally just start dragging and dropping in footage and begin learning that craft of editing. It's also free, so there's very little friction. You can just learn the skill of editing and even if you have an older Mac or older computer, it is still going to run really well cuz it's so simple. So I think this is perfect if you're just trying to learn the basics, cutting out your mistakes, adding in some B-roll. You can do all of that inside iMovie and it won't overwhelm you. And I think that makes iMovie unique is the magnetic timeline. So as you begin to edit and reorder clips, everything will kind of snap to the beginning so you don't have this big empty gap in the middle. This is a little bit different compared to more pro level editing software that doesn't have this magnetic timeline. Basically, if you delete a clip, everything stays in place. And depending on your preference, you might prefer one or the other. Some people love this feature as it allows them to edit quickly, but if you are more familiar with the non-magnetic timeline, it could be an annoyance or kind of restrictive. But the downsides of iMovie is you will eventually outgrow it because it has very limited effects. There's very basic transitions. I find that you there's not a lot of color grading tools. You can basically do some contrast and exposure, but nothing really creative if you really want to experiment with developing a look to your video. But again, if you're that type of person who's never edited before, I think iMovie is a great way to get started and that's how I got my start as well. Now if you want something that lets you do a little bit more than iMovie would do without feeling too overwhelming, then CapCut would be our next recommendation. And it's super popular. A lot of TikTok editors will use CapCut mobile, which is a great mobile app, but they also have a desktop version that you can use for your long-form style YouTube videos or podcast, etc. And CapCut's going to get you a lot more power and effects and features than iMovie would, but it's still in a pretty user-friendly design and for the most part it's actually free to use as well. What I like about CapCut over iMovie is it just has a lot of cool built-in effects like background removal. So if you want to put yourself over a clean background or add some captions, there's a lot of cool pro level effects they can just do with a press of a button, which is really cool. And it's also available on not just Mac, you can do on Windows as well. And even though CapCut is designed for beginners, even pros use CapCut. We work with freelance editors who use CapCut and there's just a lot like professional level tools that you can use even though the software feels like it's maybe more geared towards beginners, pros can use it as well. I think advantage that CapCut has is it's just very simple and intuitive. So if you haven't edited before, things are pretty simple and you know, things that are labeled make sense. A lot of pro editing software can be very confusing. So I think CapCut is just a little bit more beginner-friendly, but it does have some limitations too. For example, some advanced effects are locked behind the paid version, so you have to pay a subscription to unlock some of those features. They're also not as customizable as some professional editors that allow you control the color grading or even multi-cam. I find that CapCut doesn't do that as well if you're trying to switch between angles. So you might want to get more professional editing software if you plan on doing that. But overall, if you're looking for something very basic and not getting overwhelmed and you just want to edit for yourself, I think CapCut is a great option and there is a free version if you want to get started. >> Now if you feel like you're starting to outgrow some of these entry-level softwares like iMovie and CapCut, that's where there are some more professional level softwares. And there's three main programs that creators can currently consider. The top three are Final Cut Pro, Adobe Premiere Pro, and DaVinci Resolve. And each one of these has their own strengths and weaknesses. So let's break down who each one of these programs is actually for. And let's start talking about Final Cut Pro here. And this is designed for Mac computers. So PC users will not have access to Final Cut Pro. But there's a lot of benefits to actually going with Final Cut Pro and I started on iMovie and then I made the leap to Final Cut and used that for probably about two to three years as my core workhorse editing program and then I switched. Now I edit in Adobe Premiere. But Final Cut is really best for creators who want speed and simplicity still, but they want pro level features like multi-cam workflows, better color corrections, effects and transitions. And actually within the last few months, Final Cut Pro had a pretty major upgrade and for years it was Final Cut Pro X, but they just released Final Cut Pro 11 in 2025. And it's a pretty solid upgrade and Final Cut Pro users were stoked when it was released. They added features like a magnetic masking tool, which is almost like AI rotoscoping. So you don't have to like rotoscope or like outline different text or like if you wanted like a car driving through a scene or something, it'll like automatically detect that vehicle and then you can add like effects or color grades to that thing using AI. They added automatic captioning as well using AI tools and lots of different upgrades to make Final Cut Pro 11 a really competitive software this year. I think there are a couple benefits with Final Cut Pro in particular. Number one, it's very fast if you have a Mac, it's optimized for that. So things will feel very snappy. It's also a simple interface. So again, like iMovie, how it's very basic, this brings you into a more professional application, but still things are really simple. And then the other thing I really like about it is it's a one-time price. So it's $300 one-time fee. You're not paying a subscription. Or if you have the EDU pricing, it's even cheaper at $200. So if you are trying to learn a program, this is a great even as a gift to someone who's wanting to learn editing. So definitely recommend that. And they also have a 90-day free trial. So if you even just want to start editing for 3 months, you know, you can get started and then if you feel like it's worth the upgrade, you can buy it and have a great editing software to start making great videos. And of course, we want to talk about the downsides of each program. With Final Cut Pro, I find that you often need a lot of plugins to really make it work for you. So sometimes you might have to download an adjustment layer to do your effects on. And there's just not as many tools as something like Premiere or DaVinci Resolve has if you want a lot of control. Color grading is another thing. If you really want to push your color grades, you know, Final Cut has more tools of course than CapCut and iMovie, but not as much as some of the other options. And of course, the magnetic timeline could be a little bit limiting if you want a more creative and loose form of editing and want to like keep things kind of almost organized in such a way. Yeah, for me it was a a thing of like I think of editing is almost putting a puzzle together and I like to sort of like flip over all the puzzle pieces and start like organizing them based on color like you would a real puzzle, right? You don't just like start at frame one and just like edit frame by frame all second by second all the way through the video. And to me, Final Cut is kind of set up that way where everything just snaps to the beginning of the timeline. So when you cut things out, everything just snaps. I kind of like to at times edit the end of my video first or work on the middle and then reorganize things that way, which is why I felt like Premiere was a natural change for me and more of the way my mind works. So to get into some of the virality related stuff uh because you service 300 400 people on going viral consistently. What would you say is the number one mistake people make when trying to go viral social media? >> Yeah. So people just like sometimes I always like to say everyone thinks they're a great marketer. Um but they will just post videos that they come up with and kind of expect it to go viral. Um, and then eventually when it doesn't, they'll just give up. But the thing with content is there are so many case studies out there of people in your niche that have already gone viral. And what you can do is you can just search up your niche in the Instagram explore page and you can click accounts and you can find like the top 10 to 20 people in your account or like in your niche. You go to their accounts and you basically want to look for videos like we call them outlier videos, but it's basically videos that just get a significantly amount or like a how how should I word this? >> Like a higher amount of views than their average. >> Yes. So like I I do what's called the 5x rule. So I'm like, okay, if it gets 5x views and the accounts followers, that's like a viral video in my mind. So we'll add all of those to a spreadsheet from their page. And then we will write out like the hooks. We'll write out how many views it got, the topic of the video, like the format it was filmed in. Um, and we'll do that for like 10 to 20 videos for each creator, um, for those 10 to 20 creators. So, you're getting a list of like few hundred reels that are like the best performing in your niche. And then you just use the hooks and the topics from those videos in your own content, but make the value your expertise and like your actual actionable valuable advice. And that is what works. And even still like when you're starting a new account, you can do that. But the first little bit you just have to like push through and get through because it's a slow growth like when you are starting a new account, but if you stay consistent and you do that method, you will grow a lot faster than just trying to post whatever you want to do. >> And what have you learned about social media virality uh like in the past few months that maybe you didn't realize a year ago, two years ago? Like what what's been new or has everything kind of been the same for a while? I would say obviously content's always changing. Um, but I would say probably the importance of like the three main hooks. So there's like visual hooks, so what you see in the first 3 seconds. There's written hooks, what you read in the first 3 seconds of a video. And then there's the verbal hook, so what someone says in the first 3 seconds. So using all three hooks and optimizing those. And then also it's something we call formats. So, like if you look at short form content, subconsciously you don't realize it when you're scrolling, but if you intentionally scroll, you'll see some people use like a whiteboard. We call that whiteboard format where they're like like maybe revealing things under paper on a whiteboard. Or we have like talking back and forth format where like two people might be dressed or one person is dressed up as two different people and they're like doing a little skit talking back and forth. Or there's talking head where you're just talking straight to the camera or there's audio B-roll where there's like a trending audio and then there's text on the screen. Um, but there's a ton of different formats and what I love to do is if I ever get a viral video, I will just recreate it in different formats and it usually does very well. >> Which of those formats would you say is the most valuable? Like if you could only be really exceptional at one um, and you wanted to use it to grow your business, what would you say is the most valuable? I would say the people who can consistently go viral and have the best personal brands like do talking form because I would say like if you look at an audio B-roll video versus a talking form video, you always get more followers, um more likes, more engagement, more leads through talking form videos, even if they get less views. Um but I would say anyone who can go viral just talking to the camera, that's like the best. um because it's the least engaging. Um so if you can do well with it, that means like what you're saying is super valuable and people don't need like all the ADHD things to like stay on your video. >> Yeah, that's interesting. I see like this big emphasis on short form content particularly among business owners where they're like, "Oh, we need to have like the best edits like um and like the best graphics, etc." But does like does editing really go far in a video? >> No. I would say like if I had to rank things, I would say it's the hook, then the script, um then the format, um and then probably the editing. >> Interesting. Okay. And then of the hooks, you said, uh visual hook, written hook, auditory hook. Uh, I would say it used to be verbal, but now it's visual because I mean people have always listened to things on mute, but now like if you're scrolling, there's so many people doing so many things and like so many visual hooks. So, you have to have a good one too if you want to make people stop. >> And what goes into a good visual hook and like what really makes people stop scrolling on Instagram? >> Yeah. So, I would say there's a ton of different visual hooks and like some people think like, "Oh, I have to do something crazy." But you can literally it can just be like you sitting down in a chair, like just a motion or like what we do a lot for clients is like if we're doing like a personal trainer. This is how my client went from 150 to 130 lbs in XYZ time and like have actual like pictures of the client's before and after on the screen. Um, so that could be a visual hook. um or like a above angle. So it's like the camera's up here instead of just like right here. So it doesn't have to be that complicated, but we just usually um when we're scrolling, if we ever see a new one that follows the 5x rule, we'll just add it to our little sheet. But there's just a ton you can do. >> And for you specifically, because I would imagine when you do this type of service business, you can't teach someone all the factors that go into a video. Like you can give them the script, the research, maybe an idea of the format, visual hook, etc. But like, do you think about your clothes? Do you think about like your hairstyle? Do you think about like the tonality of your voice for certain things? Like how many factors and variables are at play? >> Yeah. Like I would say that's kind of the hardest part. Everyone like we kind of leave the tone and well we'll ask clients if they want like their scripts to be funnier, more professional, serious, like an authority, but tone and like how you dress. I mean, tone's important, but like how you dress um and stuff like that, like it's probably things we care about less. But when we give a client a script, we basically at the top we have like three different sections. It's like the notes section, which gives a breakdown of like what format it is and how to film that format. Then we have the script section and it's just we put the script in bullets. And how we have clients film is we're like look at a bullet and then say it to the camera and then look at the other bullet then say it to the camera and we'll cut out all the pauses and then we have the caption um which is just the caption we put on the platforms. >> And then the subtitles, how important are like good subtitles for videos? >> Um I mean I would say back in the day they were like more important but now like it just doesn't really matter. So, if it was like dog training, you just type in dog training. Or you could create like I like to create like five to 10 keywords that relate to that. And then once you search it, you click the reals tab. And I wish I could show you this visually, but then you will just come across literally like the most viral hooks in that niche. And if you like scroll on that reals tab after you do that keyword research, you want to look through all the videos that have over a million plus views and just save the hooks that you would use. And then typically what I do is I just make sure they follow the 5x rule and then I'll add them onto a spreadsheet. And then those are like I mean Instagram SEO kind of sucks but >> it really does. Yeah. >> Yeah. I mean Tik Toks is really good compared to Instagram but I don't like to use Tik Tok as a reference cuz Instagram is harder to go viral on. So like I like to use Instagram for ideiation better. But yeah that's probably the research I would do along with like finding the top 10 to 20 creators. And then after basically basically what I do for clients, I would just write probably a batch of like 15 to 30 scripts with, you know, the viral hooks, the viral topics, but with your own, this is so important. You don't want to copy viral videos. That will just make you look like a fraud. So you want to add your own like value in the meat of the video. So your own actionable advice. So, like if you're a dog trainer, you want to show people how to teach their dog step by step how to sit or like I saw this one girl do like how I taught my dog to shut the door and I thought it was like the coolest thing and she gave you the stepby-step advice. I hate or I don't hate but like when people give like fluff, it just never works. I mean, it doesn't never work, but it just is a lot harder to go viral when you're giving fluff um than opposed to like actionable value that people can actually take away right away. So write scripts with your own expertise and experience um on how to get you know people XYZ result. Make 15 scripts um use different formats. Test everything out on your account. So if you are starting from scratch, I like to try a bunch of different topics, a bunch of different formats in your niche and then you post like 50 to 100 videos. Try and post every single day. And then basically after 50 to 100 videos on a new account, you can kind of see, okay, what worked better, what formats worked better, what hook hooks worked well, what topics worked well, and then you just start doubling down on, you know, those hooks, formats, and topics. - Harassed on OnlyFans? How to Identify Trolls and Stop It Legally: Kind: captions Language: en OnlyFans has evolved into a massive creator economy. For thousands of digital entrepreneurs, the platform provides a primary income source, allowing them to build independent careers on their own terms. But this professional path often comes with a dark side. Many creators face a constant stream of harassment that ranges from unwanted explicit messages to coordinated verbal abuse. The security tools provided by the platform are often insufficient to stop a persistent attacker. Automated moderation and basic features are easily bypassed by anyone willing to simply open a new account and continue the harassment. Because these internal systems fail to deter repeat offenders, creators can find themselves trapped in a cycle of targeted abuse with no clear way to stop the person responsible. Harassment frequently migrates off the platform and onto anonymous forums like Tattle.Life. These sites allow users to post without accountability, often leading to the public exposure of a creator's private life. This is doxing, the targeted release of private information used to silence or intimidate someone. It turns a creator's personal data into a tool for harassment. In the most severe cases, this escalates into blackmail or sextortion. Abusers may threaten to send a creator's intimate content to their family members or employers unless specific demands are met. These attackers operate under the absolute belief that their digital presence is untraceable. They rely on the distance created by fake usernames and avatars to shield them from the reality of their actions. When an abuser believes they are invisible, they are much more likely to escalate from simple trolling to criminal threats, assuming they will never face a real-world response. That sense of invisibility is a mistake. The truth is that even the most cautious anonymous users leave a digital trail that can be followed back to their front door. Basic self-help tactics, like trying to reverse search an email or pleading with the attacker to stop, rarely work. These methods lack the authority needed to force a harasser out of the shadows. Ending the cycle requires moving beyond platform-level reporting. It requires specialized legal tools designed to strip away the digital layers an attacker uses to hide. By utilizing internet law, creators can bridge the gap between a screen name and a physical person, turning the attacker's own data against them. This process starts with a court order, such as a Norwich Pharmacal order. This legal mechanism compels platform administrators to disclose the specific data points they usually keep hidden. The order grants access to a first tier of evidence, the exact IP addresses used by the attacker, cross-referenced with the precise time they logged in and out of the platform. Beyond technical data, the order can reveal financial identifiers. This includes the geographic postcode associated with the credit card used to pay for the account, providing a direct link to a physical location. When digital logs are combined with financial records, the attacker's anonymity is replaced by a documented identity, leaving them personally and legally exposed. When a harasser realizes they have been identified, the power dynamic shifts immediately. The shield they relied on is gone. They are suddenly forced to consider the professional fallout. For many, the prospect of their employer discovering their behavior is a risk they are not prepared to take. There are also deep personal stakes. The threat of friends, family, or partners learning about their hidden activities creates a level of pressure that the attacker never anticipated. Confronted with the potential for total reputation loss, harassers often pivot from aggression to damage control, frequently seeking to settle the matter quickly to avoid a public court record. This legal pressure provides the leverage needed to secure the removal of harassing content and pursue financial compensation for the harm the creator has endured. Digital abuse, doxing, and blackmail are not just part of the job. They are actionable offenses that can be stopped with the right approach. Cohen Davis Solicitors specializes in these cases, using court orders to unmask trolls while prioritizing the creator's privacy throughout the entire process. If you or someone you know is being targeted by online harassment, reach out to the firm to discuss your options. Like and subscribe for more information on protecting your digital rights. Because in the modern creator economy, the ultimate power belongs to those who know how to defend themselves.
